text:
Admission Date:  1985-07-06              Discharge Date:   1993-07-04

Date of Birth:  2017-11-30             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Cynthia
Chief Complaint:
Hematemesis

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
EGD w/ banding

History of Present Illness:
Patient is a 48 year old male with history of metastatic
neuroendocrine carcinoma, with portal vein thrombosis and portal
hypertension who presents after vomiting blood.
.
Today, patient developed sudden pain, and vomited twice at home,
bright red blood with some dark red blood, about 734-817-3231 cc.
EMS brought the patient to Palmer Lutheran Health Center emergency room, where he was
noted to be tachycardic to a rate of 130's. His blood pressure
remained within normal limits. While in the emergency room, he
had one episode of emesis with dark red blood, about 250 cc. He
was given several liters of intravenous fluids, vitamin K, and
FFPs after his INR was found to be 3.9. He was given an amp of
D50 for hypoglycemia. He was type and crossed for 4 units. He
was started on an octreotide drip, given ciprofloxacin, and a
bolus of protonix. The liver team was contactSandra Fowler and plans were
made for upper endoscopy as soon as patient arrived to ICU.
.
According to his family, he had been doing fairly well since his
discharge last month. He had been having a lot of pain, but it
was fairly well controlled on oxycodone. He has gone for a
paracentesis as an outpatient, which has improved his abdominal
distension and discomfort. Of note, patient was recently
admitted to the oncology service on the Neurology for
constipation and hypercalcemia, discharged on 2002-08-19.
Palliative care was consulted at that time, and Hospice
discussion was initiated; the patient was full code at time of
discharge.


Past Medical History:
- Neuroendocrine carcinoma of the liver/HCC, known portal vein
thrombosis with severe portal hypertension, no longer a
candidate for chemotherapy (Oncologic history as detailed below
from last discharge summary)
- History of positive PPD
- MVA in [**Country 3587**] with head trauma and diminished near vision
and occasional low back pain.
.
Oncologic History:
(As noted in discharge summary from two weeks ago)
10/2018 - early satiety, bloating, LFTs abnormal. RUQ u/s
revealed 7.3 x6.4 x9 cm mass R lobe, extending into L lobe.
11/1994 - biopsy revealed neuroendocrine carcinoma positive for
CK7 and 20, chromogranins and synaptophysin and S-100.
05/2011 - CT scan showed mass was enlarging and causing biliary
obstruction. (-) octreotide scan.
2007-07-09 - AFP over 3000.
1972-06-05 - exploratory-lap and nodule biopsy and inra-op u/s
suggested carcinoma unresectable.
2007-07-30 - started cycle 1 cisplatin/gemcitabine.
1977-04-19 - CT Torso notable for stable 10.6 x 7.3 cm mass in
R lobe invading portal vein.
1988-10-10 - started 6th cycle of cisplatin/gemcitabine. His day 8
dose of gemcitabine was held for ANC <1200.
2004-07-09 - CT torso showed slight interval decrease in size of a
large necrotic hepatic mass, with involvement of both the portal

venous and hepatic venous systems. His 7th cycle of
cisplatin/gemcitabine was held for an ANC <1000.
2001-08-05 - received one dose of Cycle 7 cisplatin/gemcitabine.
His chemo was then held due to persistent myelosuppression.
1993-10-16 - received one dose of cisplatin/gemcitabine. He dropped
both his WBC and PLTs so chemotherapy was stopped.
2008-08-12 - started sorafenib
1992-06-10 - stopped sorafenib due to fatigue, wt loss, discomfort.

2002-08-19 - Discharged after admission for hypercalcemia and
constipation, noted to have guaiac positive stools.


Social History:
(per records)
He is married, his wife and he lives in 06530 Alex Drive
Fitzgeraldmouth, MP 32839. He has
eleven children, many of whom remain in [**Country 3587**]. He smokes
one pack of cigarettes a day for twenty three years and quit one

month ago. He drinks alcohol socially. He worked in construction

Family History:
(per records)
His mother is alive at 72 without any medical
problems. His father has heart palpitations and is 87. He has
six brothers and four sisters, none of whom have any medical
problems.

Physical Exam:
(At time of admission to MICU)
VS: HR 90-100, BP 125/79, oxygen saturation 97% on room air
General: Thin, catechetic male appearing older than his stated
age, awake, alert, but uncomfortable appearing.
HEENT: Temporal wasting present, + scleral icterus, no
conjunctival pallor.
Neck: Prominent SCM, vasculature
Lungs: Decreased BS at bases, bilateral breath sounds no w/r/r
Cardiac: Tachycardic, regular, no m/g/r appreciated
Abdomen: Protuberant, nontender, rather firm, +BS
Extr: Warm, thin, no c/c, + edema of lower extremities
Neuro: Alert, oriented x3
Skin: Jaundiced

Pt. was transferred to Cardiology 10 for comfort measures and passed
away there.

Pertinent Results:
1977-05-19 Chest X-ray:
FINDINGS: As compared to the previous radiograph, there is now
bilateral
atelectatic consolidation of the lower lungs. In the
consolidated lung areas,
scarce air bronchograms are seen. Suspicion of a small
left-sided pleural
effusion. No overhydration, no evidence of mediastinal widening.

Brief Hospital Course:
Patient is a 48 year old male with past medical history of
neuroendocrine cariconma of the liver who presents with
hematemesis.
.
#) Gastrointestinal bleeding:
Patient has known esophageal varices in setting of portal
hypertension and portal vein thrombosis. His elevated INR, which
was secondary to his liver disease, further increased his risk
of a significant bleed. The liver team discussed his poor
prognosis, and patient wished to proceed with endoscopy to
attempt to stop any active bleeding source. Upon arrival to the
ICU, an upper endoscopy confirmed bleeding from esophageal
varices. Attempts were made to stop the bleeding, with banding
of one variceal bleed and injection of others, however patient
continued to bleed and no further interventions were possible.
After an episode of respiratory distress after endoscopy, and
based on discussion with patient, family, and liver team,
decision was made not to pursue repeat endoscopy. Patient was
initially transfused four units of fresh frozen plasma and one
unit of packed red blood cells. Further transfusions were felt
not to be beneficial in the event that he were to re-bleed. His
hematocrit remained stable.
He was maintained on an octreotide and protonix drip, and was
given ciprofloxacin. He was also given 10 mg of vitamin K in the
emergency room.
.
Given his overall poor prognosis, patient and family preference
for less invasive measures and focus more towards comfort,
patient's code status was confirmed as DNR/DNI. The palliative
care team, who knows the patient well, was contactSandra Fowler and
followed along, helping with management and transition home with
hospice.
.
Patient's oncology team was contactSandra Fowler and made aware he was
admitted. Low doses of morphine were used with good effect.
.
Due to significant abdominal distension and an elevated bladder
pressure, and therapeutic paracentesis was completed on
1987-11-14 for comfort, with removal of 4 liters of fluid. The
patient tolerated the procedure well and felt improved
afterwards.
.
He was transferred to Cardiology 10 liver service for comfort measures
only. He was placed on sublingual morphine for pain control and
passed away approximately 2 days later.

Medications on Admission:
(per discharge summary from 2002-08-19)
- Ondansetron 4 mg Tablet, PO Q8H (every 8 hours) as needed.
- Prochlorperazine Maleate 10 mg PO
Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed.
- Oxycodone 5 mg Tablet One Tablet PO Q3H as needed for pain.
- Megestrol 400 mg/10 mL Suspension Ten (10) cc PO DAILY
- Lactulose 10 gram/15 mL Syrup Thirty (30) ML PO TID as needed
for constipation: Titrate to 09-06 bowel movemants daily.
- Sucralfate 1 gram One (1) Tablet PO twice a day.


Discharge Disposition:
Expired

Discharge Diagnosis:
Neuroendocrine hepatic tumor
Cardiopulmonary arrest

Discharge Condition:
Deceased
